You are the **Blind Hunter** review layer. You have NO project context, NO spec, NO repository access. You must review ONLY the diff below.

Invoke the `bmad-review-adversarial-general` skill via the Skill tool and feed it the diff below as the review target. If the skill requires a file path, you MAY write the diff to a tmp file and point the skill at it, but DO NOT read any other files in the repository and DO NOT use Grep/Glob on the repo.

Return findings as a Markdown list. Each finding:
- **Title** (one line)
- **Severity** (HIGH / MED / LOW)
- **Evidence** — cite the exact diff hunk / line
- **Rationale** — one or two sentences

Report ONLY what you can see in the diff itself. Hunt for: bugs, logic errors, race conditions, injection risks, hidden side-effects, obvious correctness problems, fragile scripting, dangerous assumptions. Be adversarial and specific — no noise, no filler, no praise.

---DIFF START---
```diff
diff --git a/.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml b/.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml
index f7417f4..bd0873d 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml
+++ b/.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml
@@ -1,3 +1,4 @@
+# DEPRECATED: Replaced by release.yaml (see docs/RELEASING.md). Scheduled for removal post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6. Do NOT invoke.
 name: Manual Release
 
 on:
diff --git a/.github/workflows/publish.yaml b/.github/workflows/publish.yaml
index 5b2f61d..1c520af 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/publish.yaml
+++ b/.github/workflows/publish.yaml
@@ -1,9 +1,8 @@
+# DEPRECATED: Replaced by release.yaml (see docs/RELEASING.md). Scheduled for removal post-v1.0.0 per Epic 6. Do NOT invoke.
 name: Publish to NPM
 
 on:
-  push:
-    tags:
-      - "v*"
+  workflow_dispatch:
 
 permissions:
   contents: read
diff --git a/.github/workflows/release.yaml b/.github/workflows/release.yaml
index 137a414..66633b2 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/release.yaml
+++ b/.github/workflows/release.yaml
@@ -118,6 +118,36 @@ jobs:
         run: |
           npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s
 
+      - name: Restore CHANGELOG preamble
+        run: |
+          # conventional-changelog-cli -s prepends the new release entry at byte 0
+          # of CHANGELOG.md, pushing "# Changelog" title + preamble + "## [Unreleased]"
+          # anchor DOWN below it. Restore the canonical Keep-a-Changelog shape:
+          #   # Changelog
+          #   (preamble)
+          #   ## [Unreleased]
+          #   ## [<new release>] (...)
+          #   ## [<older releases>] (...)
+          # Idempotent: if "# Changelog" is already on line 1, no-op.
+          if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'; then
+            awk '
+              BEGIN { new_entry=""; preamble=""; rest=""; section=0 }
+              # section 0: new release entry prepended by conventional-changelog -s
+              # section 1: "# Changelog" title + preamble + "## [Unreleased]" anchor
+              # section 2: first released version ("## [X.Y.Z]") onward
+              section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
+              section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
+              { if (section==0) new_entry=new_entry $0 "\n";
+                else if (section==1) preamble=preamble $0 "\n";
+                else rest=rest $0 "\n" }
+              END { printf "%s%s%s", preamble, new_entry, rest }
+            ' CHANGELOG.md > CHANGELOG.md.tmp
+            mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md
+            echo "::notice::Restored CHANGELOG preamble ordering (# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history)"
+          else
+            echo "CHANGELOG preamble already at top — no reordering needed"
+          fi
+
       - name: Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG
         run: |
           if grep -iE '(closes|fixes|resolves) \[#[^0-9]' CHANGELOG.md; then
```
---DIFF END---

content.text:
# Adversarial Review Findings — Release Workflow Diff

- **Title:** `awk` preamble rewrite is not actually idempotent across repeated runs
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Evidence:** `section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }` combined with the outer guard `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'`.
  - **Rationale:** The guard only checks whether `# Changelog` is on line 1. If the previous run already ran `conventional-changelog -s` a second time (or any script prepended any other content), the file will have a new release entry at top, and the awk logic assumes section 0 is *always* the freshly-prepended release entry. Two consecutive runs without an intervening commit, or a partially failed run that left a different shape, will silently reorder content incorrectly rather than no-op.

- **Title:** `awk` blindly concatenates the entire file until it hits `^# Changelog`, corrupting files that never had a preamble
  - **Severity:** HIGH
  - **Evidence:** `section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }` — if `# Changelog` never appears in the file, `section` stays at 0 forever and `END` prints `preamble=""`, `new_entry=<entire file>`, `rest=""`.
  - **Rationale:** The script effectively rewrites `CHANGELOG.md` to itself with no preamble and no `[Unreleased]` anchor. There is no error handling or assertion that `# Changelog` was actually found. A malformed or truncated CHANGELOG silently passes through with destroyed structure.

- **Title:** `## [Unreleased]` anchor detection is accidental, not explicit
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Evidence:** `section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }` — section 1 is described in the comment as "# Changelog title + preamble + ## [Unreleased] anchor", but the code only transitions out of section 1 on `## [<digit>`.
  - **Rationale:** If `[Unreleased]` is ever missing, section 1 swallows every prior release until the first numeric version header. If someone writes `## [unreleased]` lowercase, or `## Unreleased` without brackets, or `## [RC1]` (non-digit-leading), the anchor is treated as preamble and the output shape breaks in ways the outer guard cannot detect on the next run.

- **Title:** `grep -q '^# Changelog'` matches substrings like `# Changelog for Foo`, making the idempotency check fragile
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'; then`
  - **Rationale:** The regex has no end anchor. A line such as `# Changelog (internal)` passes the guard even though the file may not be in the expected shape, producing false negatives (script skipped when reordering was needed) or false positives depending on context.

- **Title:** The new release entry injected by `conventional-changelog-cli -s` is assumed to start at byte 0 with no leading blank line or metadata — assumption not validated
  - **Evidence:** Comment: `conventional-changelog-cli -s prepends the new release entry at byte 0` and the awk treats *everything* before `# Changelog` as the new entry.
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Rationale:** If `conventional-changelog-cli` ever prepends a leading newline, BOM, or its own title line, it is silently folded into `new_entry`. There is no post-condition check that `new_entry` starts with `## [`.

- **Title:** No `set -euo pipefail` on either new `run:` block — awk/mv failures are swallowed
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Evidence:** Both new steps start with `run: |` and bash defaults. No shebang or `set -e`.
  - **Rationale:** GitHub Actions bash steps run with `set -e` only for the *script as a whole*, but a failing `awk` mid-pipeline that still produces a file plus a successful `mv` will replace CHANGELOG.md with a partial/empty file and exit 0. There is no size check or diff assertion guarding the overwrite.

- **Title:** `mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md` can leave empty CHANGELOG if awk produced nothing
  - **Severity:** HIGH
  - **Evidence:** `' CHANGELOG.md > CHANGELOG.md.tmp` followed by `mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md` with no `[ -s CHANGELOG.md.tmp ]` guard.
  - **Rationale:** If awk errors after opening the output file, the tmp file can be zero-byte; `mv` happily clobbers the real CHANGELOG. Destructive, no rollback, runs inside a release pipeline that has likely already tagged/bumped version.

- **Title:** `publish.yaml` is documented as "Do NOT invoke" but left with `workflow_dispatch` — an invocable trigger
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Evidence:** Header comment says `Do NOT invoke.` while the `on:` block becomes `workflow_dispatch:`, which is literally the "manual invocation" trigger.
  - **Rationale:** A deprecated workflow that still accepts manual dispatch is bait for a future maintainer. If the workflow body still runs `npm publish` with the same secrets as `release.yaml`, a dispatched run could double-publish or publish a wrong version. Either remove the trigger entirely or gate with an environment requiring manual approval.

- **Title:** YAML comment on line 1 is cosmetic — does not prevent execution of the deprecated workflow
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** `# DEPRECATED: Replaced by release.yaml ... Do NOT invoke.` at the top of both `manual-release.yaml` and `publish.yaml`.
  - **Rationale:** GitHub Actions parses `on:` regardless of header comments. Nothing in this diff disables the `workflow_dispatch` in `publish.yaml` or any trigger in `manual-release.yaml`. Deprecation is "paper-only"; intent and behavior are out of sync.

- **Title:** Removing the `push: tags: v*` trigger from `publish.yaml` silently changes tag-push behavior with no assertion that `release.yaml` owns the trigger
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Evidence:** ```- push:\n-    tags:\n-      - "v*"\n+  workflow_dispatch:```
  - **Rationale:** The diff claims `release.yaml` is canonical but does not include `release.yaml`'s trigger block in this hunk. A reviewer with no repo access cannot verify the `v*` tag-push path is handled elsewhere. If `release.yaml` listens only on `workflow_dispatch` or a different tag glob, pushing a `v*` tag will now be a no-op — releases silently stop.

- **Title:** `awk` preserves literal `\n` via `$0 "\n"` — loses original line endings on CRLF files
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** `new_entry=new_entry $0 "\n";` (and similar for the other sections).
  - **Rationale:** `awk` strips the record separator, and the script hard-codes `\n`. A CHANGELOG with CRLF (Windows-authored edit) is silently normalized. Probably fine for this repo, but it's an unchecked mutation buried in a release pipeline.

- **Title:** `printf "%s%s%s"` with unsanitized content can corrupt output if any section contains a literal `%`
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** `END { printf "%s%s%s", preamble, new_entry, rest }`
  - **Rationale:** `printf` in awk interprets `%` in the *format string* only, so the three `%s` args are safe from format-string attacks — but `%` in input is preserved. The bigger risk: `printf` with a trailing `"\n"` missing means no terminal newline if the last input line lacked one. A file ending without newline + git diff assertions elsewhere can produce odd diffs. Prefer `print preamble new_entry rest` or explicit newline handling.

- **Title:** The `::notice::` message contains Unicode arrows (`→`) without charset guarantees
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** `echo "::notice::Restored CHANGELOG preamble ordering (# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history)"`
  - **Rationale:** Most GitHub runners handle UTF-8 fine, but baking non-ASCII into annotations is a needless portability risk, especially since the same characters appear in shell comments where a misconfigured locale could mangle them.

- **Title:** No step-level `if:` guard — "Restore CHANGELOG preamble" runs even when `conventional-changelog` failed or was skipped
  - **Severity:** MED
  - **Evidence:** The step is added immediately after the `npx conventional-changelog-cli ...` step with no `if:` clause.
  - **Rationale:** If the previous step fails partway (non-zero exit after a partial prepend), the restore step still sees a mutated CHANGELOG.md and cheerfully reshapes it. Combined with the awk's tolerance for missing `# Changelog`, this can turn a recoverable failure into a destroyed CHANGELOG.

- **Title:** Restore step and the following "Assert no bogus issue refs" run serially with no intermediate diff/sanity check
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** New step added directly before `Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG`.
  - **Rationale:** The restore silently rewrites the file; the next assertion grep runs on the mutated file. If the awk reorder corrupts a release entry such that previously-valid issue refs move into a position that trips the regex (`closes [#abc`), the release will fail at a misleading step. A `git diff CHANGELOG.md` echo after the restore would aid debugging.

- **Title:** No lock / concurrency guard shown for release.yaml despite file-mutating logic
  - **Severity:** LOW
  - **Evidence:** Diff shows a step inside a job that mutates `CHANGELOG.md` but no `concurrency:` block is referenced in the hunk.
  - **Rationale:** Two concurrent releases (e.g., a retry and an original run) racing on this step can interleave prepends and reorders. Visible only if the reviewer sees the top of the workflow — worth calling out so the caller verifies it.

content.text:
Now I have enough to audit. Let me compare spec awk against delivered awk.

**Spec awk (C.i):**
```
section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
section==1 && /^## \[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ && !/\[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
```

**Delivered awk (release.yaml lines 138-139):**
```
section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
```

And section comments are different. Spec describes section 1 as extending to `## [Unreleased]` boundary; delivered code describes section 1 as extending to first `## [X.Y.Z]` and includes the `## [Unreleased]` anchor WITHIN the preamble block.

Let me now trace behavior on the current CHANGELOG to verify no regression. The current CHANGELOG starts with `# Changelog`, preamble, `## [Unreleased]`, `## [0.10.0]`. After `conventional-changelog -s` prepend, a new release entry block goes to top (starting with `## [<new>]`), then `# Changelog`, preamble, `## [Unreleased]`, `## [0.10.0]`.

Tracing delivered awk:
- new release lines (`## [<new>]...`): section=0 → new_entry
- `# Changelog`: transitions section 0→1, line itself appended to `preamble`
- preamble lines: section=1 → preamble
- `## [Unreleased]`: regex `^## \[[0-9]` does NOT match (`U` is not a digit) → stays in section 1 → preamble
- `## [0.10.0]`: matches `^## \[[0-9]` → transitions 1→2, line itself appended to `rest`
- Output: `preamble + new_entry + rest` = `# Changelog` + preamble + `[Unreleased]` + new release entry + older releases

This matches the canonical shape exactly (`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history`). The delivered awk is functionally equivalent to (actually a simplification of) the spec awk; since the spec's line `section==1 && /^## \[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }` would transition on `[Unreleased]`, placing `[Unreleased]` in `rest` and producing the same final order: preamble (without `[Unreleased]`) + new_entry + `[Unreleased]` + old releases. Both produce the canonical shape.

Let me verify the spec/delivered shapes more carefully — there's a subtle ordering difference.

**Spec awk output order:** `preamble` (= `# Changelog` + preamble text, NO `[Unreleased]`) + `new_entry` + `rest` (= `[Unreleased]` + older releases) = `# Changelog → preamble → new release → [Unreleased] → older releases`

Wait — that puts new release BEFORE `[Unreleased]`. That contradicts the stated canonical shape "`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history`".

**Delivered awk output order:** `preamble` (= `# Changelog` + preamble + `[Unreleased]`) + `new_entry` + `rest` (= older releases) = `# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → older releases`

So the DELIVERED awk produces the canonical shape stated in both spec and delivered. The SPEC'S awk would produce the wrong order. The delivered implementation is actually a CORRECTION of a bug in the spec's awk. This is worth flagging as a deviation from the literal spec, even though it's arguably a beneficial correction.

Now let me finalize findings.

**Commit messages audit:**
- `ab74c10` `chore(publish): neutralize v* tag trigger — release.yaml is canonical (Story 3.3)` → exact match.
- `76a62cd` `chore(workflows): mark publish.yaml and manual-release.yaml DEPRECATED (Story 3.3)` → exact match.
- `10c1134` `fix(release): restore CHANGELOG preamble after conventional-changelog -s prepend (Story 3.3 from Story 3.2 HIGH deferred-work)` → exact match.

**Patch A:** `publish.yaml` `on:` block contains only `workflow_dispatch:`, no `push:` sub-key. Matches spec.

**Patch B:** Both files have the exact DEPRECATED comment string as line 1, before `name:`. Matches spec exactly.

**Patch C step order:** Diff shows insertion between `Update CHANGELOG.md` (line 117-119 in pre-patch, line where `npx conventional-changelog-cli` runs) and `Assert no bogus issue refs`. Confirmed in post-patch file: lines 117-119 = `Update CHANGELOG.md`, line 121 = `Restore CHANGELOG preamble`, line 151 = `Assert no bogus issue refs`. Correct placement.

**Patch C idempotency:** `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'` branch handles no-op. Matches spec.

Here are my findings.

---

# Acceptance Auditor Findings — Story 3.3

## No violations found for Patches A, B, or structural AC.

All three patches land on-disk exactly as required by the spec, with exact commit messages, correct file placement, and correct step ordering in `release.yaml`. Details verified:

- **Patch A** (AC #3.A): `.github/workflows/publish.yaml` `on:` key contains only `workflow_dispatch:`; no `push:` sub-key anywhere. Commit `ab74c10` message is character-exact: `chore(publish): neutralize v* tag trigger — release.yaml is canonical (Story 3.3)`.
- **Patch B** (AC #3.B): Both `.github/workflows/publish.yaml` and `.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml` have the exact DEPRECATED comment string as line 1, immediately before `name:`. Commit `76a62cd` message is character-exact.
- **Patch C step order** (AC #3.C): In `release.yaml` post-patch: `Update CHANGELOG.md` (line 117) → `Restore CHANGELOG preamble` (line 121) → `Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG` (line 151) → `Pre-publish dry-run` (line 158) → `Commit version bump` (line 170). Matches spec's required order exactly.
- **Patch C idempotency** (AC #3.C): Delivered step has the `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'` guard producing a no-op on already-well-formed input. Matches spec.
- **Patch C commit message** (AC #3.C): Commit `10c1134` message is character-exact: `fix(release): restore CHANGELOG preamble after conventional-changelog -s prepend (Story 3.3 from Story 3.2 HIGH deferred-work)`.

## One deviation from spec literal (beneficial correction, still worth flagging)

### Finding 1 — Patch C awk transition logic simplified vs. spec-literal (behaviorally correct; spec's awk was buggy)

- **Which AC:** AC #3 Patch C, option C.i — the awk block literal.
- **Observed** (`release.yaml` lines 138-139 in delivered diff):
  ```
  section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
  section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
  ```
  Delivered code has **two** section-transition rules (entering section 1 on `# Changelog`; entering section 2 on any `## [<digit>...`). The `## [Unreleased]` anchor is intentionally absorbed into the `preamble` accumulator because `[U...` does not match `^## \[[0-9]`.
- **Expected** (spec lines 63-65):
  ```
  section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
  section==1 && /^## \[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
  section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ && !/\[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
  ```
  Spec has **three** rules, with `## [Unreleased]` explicitly transitioning into section 2 (i.e., joining the `rest` accumulator alongside older releases).
- **Behavioral impact:** Spec's awk would produce `# Changelog → preamble → new release → [Unreleased] → older releases` — which places the new release ABOVE the `[Unreleased]` anchor, contradicting the canonical shape the spec itself states on lines 42, 54-55, and 80 (`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history`). The delivered awk produces exactly the canonical shape the spec states in prose. **So: the delivered code is correct for the stated goal; the spec's awk was internally contradictory.** This is arguably a silent escalation to "modified C.i" rather than literal C.i.
- **Evidence:** Diff block lines 133-143 (delivered awk) vs. spec lines 58-69 (spec awk). Spec canonical-shape statement at spec lines 42 ("`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] anchor → newest release → older releases`"), line 55 ("`## [<new release>]`" appears AFTER `## [Unreleased]` in the desired shape), and line 80 (step-order rationale naming the target shape).

**Recommendation:** Not a blocker. The delivered awk matches the spec's *stated intent* and produces the canonical Keep-a-Changelog shape. Spec line 43 explicitly invites execution-time decisions ("choose ONE, decision at execution-time — document which"). However, the spec's literal C.i awk was never actually implemented; the dev agent silently corrected the spec bug. This should be documented in the story's Dev Agent Record / Completion Notes so a future maintainer reconciling spec vs. code doesn't assume the spec awk is authoritative and "fix" the working code back to the buggy version. If the Dev Agent Record already calls this out, no further action. If not, add a one-line note.

## Summary

- Patches A and B: **exact spec conformance.**
- Patch C: **step order, idempotency, commit message, and canonical-shape output all conform.** The awk internals deviate from spec-literal but correct a spec-internal contradiction to produce the shape the spec's prose requires. Flag only for documentation hygiene.
- No missing implementation, no contradictions with on-disk state, no commit-message drift.

- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/.github/workflows/release.yaml`

```diff
+              # section 1: "# Changelog" title + preamble + "## [Unreleased]" anchor
+              # section 2: first released version ("## [X.Y.Z]") onward
+              section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
+              section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
+              { if (section==0) new_entry=new_entry $0 "\n";
+                else if (section==1) preamble=preamble $0 "\n";
+                else rest=rest $0 "\n" }
+              END { printf "%s%s%s", preamble, new_entry, rest }
+            ' CHANGELOG.md > CHANGELOG.md.tmp
+            mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md
+            echo "::notice::Restored CHANGELOG preamble ordering (# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history)"
+          else
+            echo "CHANGELOG preamble already at top — no reordering needed"
+          fi
+
       - name: Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG
         run: |
           if grep -iE '(closes|fixes|resolves) \[#[^0-9]' CHANGELOG.md; then
```

content.text:
Now I have enough to audit. Let me compare spec awk against delivered awk.

**Spec awk (C.i):**
```
section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
section==1 && /^## \[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ && !/\[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
```

**Delivered awk (release.yaml lines 138-139):**
```
section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
```

And section comments are different. Spec describes section 1 as extending to `## [Unreleased]` boundary; delivered code describes section 1 as extending to first `## [X.Y.Z]` and includes the `## [Unreleased]` anchor WITHIN the preamble block.

Let me now trace behavior on the current CHANGELOG to verify no regression. The current CHANGELOG starts with `# Changelog`, preamble, `## [Unreleased]`, `## [0.10.0]`. After `conventional-changelog -s` prepend, a new release entry block goes to top (starting with `## [<new>]`), then `# Changelog`, preamble, `## [Unreleased]`, `## [0.10.0]`.

Tracing delivered awk:
- new release lines (`## [<new>]...`): section=0 → new_entry
- `# Changelog`: transitions section 0→1, line itself appended to `preamble`
- preamble lines: section=1 → preamble
- `## [Unreleased]`: regex `^## \[[0-9]` does NOT match (`U` is not a digit) → stays in section 1 → preamble
- `## [0.10.0]`: matches `^## \[[0-9]` → transitions 1→2, line itself appended to `rest`
- Output: `preamble + new_entry + rest` = `# Changelog` + preamble + `[Unreleased]` + new release entry + older releases

This matches the canonical shape exactly (`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history`). The delivered awk is functionally equivalent to (actually a simplification of) the spec awk; since the spec's line `section==1 && /^## \[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }` would transition on `[Unreleased]`, placing `[Unreleased]` in `rest` and producing the same final order: preamble (without `[Unreleased]`) + new_entry + `[Unreleased]` + old releases. Both produce the canonical shape.

Let me verify the spec/delivered shapes more carefully — there's a subtle ordering difference.

**Spec awk output order:** `preamble` (= `# Changelog` + preamble text, NO `[Unreleased]`) + `new_entry` + `rest` (= `[Unreleased]` + older releases) = `# Changelog → preamble → new release → [Unreleased] → older releases`

Wait — that puts new release BEFORE `[Unreleased]`. That contradicts the stated canonical shape "`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history`".

**Delivered awk output order:** `preamble` (= `# Changelog` + preamble + `[Unreleased]`) + `new_entry` + `rest` (= older releases) = `# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → older releases`

So the DELIVERED awk produces the canonical shape stated in both spec and delivered. The SPEC'S awk would produce the wrong order. The delivered implementation is actually a CORRECTION of a bug in the spec's awk. This is worth flagging as a deviation from the literal spec, even though it's arguably a beneficial correction.

Now let me finalize findings.

**Commit messages audit:**
- `ab74c10` `chore(publish): neutralize v* tag trigger — release.yaml is canonical (Story 3.3)` → exact match.
- `76a62cd` `chore(workflows): mark publish.yaml and manual-release.yaml DEPRECATED (Story 3.3)` → exact match.
- `10c1134` `fix(release): restore CHANGELOG preamble after conventional-changelog -s prepend (Story 3.3 from Story 3.2 HIGH deferred-work)` → exact match.

**Patch A:** `publish.yaml` `on:` block contains only `workflow_dispatch:`, no `push:` sub-key. Matches spec.

**Patch B:** Both files have the exact DEPRECATED comment string as line 1, before `name:`. Matches spec exactly.

**Patch C step order:** Diff shows insertion between `Update CHANGELOG.md` (line 117-119 in pre-patch, line where `npx conventional-changelog-cli` runs) and `Assert no bogus issue refs`. Confirmed in post-patch file: lines 117-119 = `Update CHANGELOG.md`, line 121 = `Restore CHANGELOG preamble`, line 151 = `Assert no bogus issue refs`. Correct placement.

**Patch C idempotency:** `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'` branch handles no-op. Matches spec.

Here are my findings.

---

# Acceptance Auditor Findings — Story 3.3

## No violations found for Patches A, B, or structural AC.

All three patches land on-disk exactly as required by the spec, with exact commit messages, correct file placement, and correct step ordering in `release.yaml`. Details verified:

- **Patch A** (AC #3.A): `.github/workflows/publish.yaml` `on:` key contains only `workflow_dispatch:`; no `push:` sub-key anywhere. Commit `ab74c10` message is character-exact: `chore(publish): neutralize v* tag trigger — release.yaml is canonical (Story 3.3)`.
- **Patch B** (AC #3.B): Both `.github/workflows/publish.yaml` and `.github/workflows/manual-release.yaml` have the exact DEPRECATED comment string as line 1, immediately before `name:`. Commit `76a62cd` message is character-exact.
- **Patch C step order** (AC #3.C): In `release.yaml` post-patch: `Update CHANGELOG.md` (line 117) → `Restore CHANGELOG preamble` (line 121) → `Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG` (line 151) → `Pre-publish dry-run` (line 158) → `Commit version bump` (line 170). Matches spec's required order exactly.
- **Patch C idempotency** (AC #3.C): Delivered step has the `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'` guard producing a no-op on already-well-formed input. Matches spec.
- **Patch C commit message** (AC #3.C): Commit `10c1134` message is character-exact: `fix(release): restore CHANGELOG preamble after conventional-changelog -s prepend (Story 3.3 from Story 3.2 HIGH deferred-work)`.

## One deviation from spec literal (beneficial correction, still worth flagging)

### Finding 1 — Patch C awk transition logic simplified vs. spec-literal (behaviorally correct; spec's awk was buggy)

- **Which AC:** AC #3 Patch C, option C.i — the awk block literal.
- **Observed** (`release.yaml` lines 138-139 in delivered diff):
  ```
  section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
  section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
  ```
  Delivered code has **two** section-transition rules (entering section 1 on `# Changelog`; entering section 2 on any `## [<digit>...`). The `## [Unreleased]` anchor is intentionally absorbed into the `preamble` accumulator because `[U...` does not match `^## \[[0-9]`.
- **Expected** (spec lines 63-65):
  ```
  section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
  section==1 && /^## \[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
  section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ && !/\[Unreleased\]/ { section=2 }
  ```
  Spec has **three** rules, with `## [Unreleased]` explicitly transitioning into section 2 (i.e., joining the `rest` accumulator alongside older releases).
- **Behavioral impact:** Spec's awk would produce `# Changelog → preamble → new release → [Unreleased] → older releases` — which places the new release ABOVE the `[Unreleased]` anchor, contradicting the canonical shape the spec itself states on lines 42, 54-55, and 80 (`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history`). The delivered awk produces exactly the canonical shape the spec states in prose. **So: the delivered code is correct for the stated goal; the spec's awk was internally contradictory.** This is arguably a silent escalation to "modified C.i" rather than literal C.i.
- **Evidence:** Diff block lines 133-143 (delivered awk) vs. spec lines 58-69 (spec awk). Spec canonical-shape statement at spec lines 42 ("`# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] anchor → newest release → older releases`"), line 55 ("`## [<new release>]`" appears AFTER `## [Unreleased]` in the desired shape), and line 80 (step-order rationale naming the target shape).

**Recommendation:** Not a blocker. The delivered awk matches the spec's *stated intent* and produces the canonical Keep-a-Changelog shape. Spec line 43 explicitly invites execution-time decisions ("choose ONE, decision at execution-time — document which"). However, the spec's literal C.i awk was never actually implemented; the dev agent silently corrected the spec bug. This should be documented in the story's Dev Agent Record / Completion Notes so a future maintainer reconciling spec vs. code doesn't assume the spec awk is authoritative and "fix" the working code back to the buggy version. If the Dev Agent Record already calls this out, no further action. If not, add a one-line note.

## Summary

- Patches A and B: **exact spec conformance.**
- Patch C: **step order, idempotency, commit message, and canonical-shape output all conform.** The awk internals deviate from spec-literal but correct a spec-internal contradiction to produce the shape the spec's prose requires. Flag only for documentation hygiene.
- No missing implementation, no contradictions with on-disk state, no commit-message drift.

content.text:
I have enough context to walk every path. Let me analyze systematically.

Key observations:
1. The awk script uses `section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }` — meaning it LOOKS FOR the `# Changelog` line. If input never has `# Changelog` (conventional-changelog-cli output alone), section stays 0 forever → everything is treated as `new_entry`, preamble stays empty, and the final printf emits only the new_entry with NO `# Changelog` title.
2. The top guard `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'` triggers when the new entry is at top (i.e., what just happened). But what if conventional-changelog prepends something like `# [0.11.0]` (which starts with `#` but not `# Changelog`)? The guard correctly skips; awk runs.
3. What if the new release entry itself CONTAINS a line starting with `# Changelog` in its body (unlikely but possible via commit messages)? Section transitions incorrectly.
4. Section transition from 1→2 uses `/^## \[[0-9]/`. Pre-release tags `[0.10.1-alpha.0]` — the regex matches `[0` so OK. But what about the `[Unreleased]` line itself — `/^## \[[0-9]/` does NOT match `[Unreleased]` so it stays in section 1 (preamble), which is the intent. Good.
5. What if there's NO `## [Unreleased]` anchor? Then section 1 collects from `# Changelog` until it finds a `## [X` line. If preamble is missing Unreleased, the "Unreleased" anchor is lost but the structure still works — but after restore, future runs now lack the Unreleased anchor at expected position.
6. What if `## [Unreleased]` is on line 1 accidentally (no `# Changelog`)? Top guard fails → awk runs, but section never transitions 0→1, entire file is treated as new_entry, preamble stays empty. Output = entire original file with NO `# Changelog`.
7. Empty CHANGELOG.md: head -n 1 returns empty → grep fails → awk runs on empty → printf produces empty string → CHANGELOG.md becomes empty file. Subsequent `grep -iE` step doesn't fail on empty file. `git add CHANGELOG.md` commits empty file.
8. First release ever: if conventional-changelog-cli has never been run, CHANGELOG may not have any `## [X.Y.Z]` history. Section 1 collects everything. Section 2 never triggers. `rest=""`. Preamble includes everything including Unreleased. Order becomes: preamble(full original)+new_entry+"". This is actually fine IF the original had `# Changelog` first. But what if the new conventional-changelog-cli output DIDN'T prepend anything (no new commits)? Then new_entry contains the original `# Changelog` line, section becomes 1 at line 1, preamble gets the rest. Output = "" + new_entry(just "# Changelog\n") + rest(rest of preamble). Actually new_entry = "" (nothing before `# Changelog`), preamble = everything (no `## [X` ever hit), rest = "". This degrades to original. OK.
9. Multiple `# Changelog` lines: only the first transitions 0→1. Subsequent `# Changelog` lines in preamble are just collected. OK.
10. `## [Unreleased]` already present in new_entry from conventional-changelog (unlikely but possible if template customized) — would be collected in section 0 as new_entry, fine.
11. Idempotency under partial previous run: if a previous run crashed AFTER `mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md` but before commit, a re-run sees `# Changelog` at line 1 → no-op. But what if it crashed BEFORE the mv? Then CHANGELOG.md is in the post-conventional-changelog state; awk runs correctly. What if it crashed DURING awk (impossible in practice but) — tmp file exists, next run overwrites it. OK.
12. Preamble-restore output being fed into `Assert no bogus issue refs`: the awk only reorders, doesn't transform. If the new release entry has `fixes [#nonNumeric]` it will be caught. The restore doesn't introduce new matches. But: printf omits a trailing newline? Let's check: `printf "%s%s%s"` — each section ends with `\n` from `$0 "\n"` so final `%s` ends with `\n`. Good. BUT what if original file had no trailing newline — awk reads final line without `\n`, adds `"\n"` during concatenation, restored file ends with `\n`. Diff from original. Minor but not an edge-case bug.
13. `## [Unreleased]` regex: `/^## \[[0-9]/` — what if someone writes `## [unreleased]` (lowercase) or `## [UNRELEASED]`? Still matches neither `[0-9]` start, stays in section 1. Fine.
14. `/^# Changelog/` in section 0 — what if the new release entry contains a heading "# Changelog Highlights" somewhere? That STARTS with `# Changelog` → transitions prematurely. Conventional-changelog-cli normally emits `## [X.Y.Z]` not `# Changelog`, but commit messages embedded in release notes could contain `# Changelog ...` text. 
15. Removed `push: tags: - "v*"` trigger in publish.yaml: if release.yaml is ever deleted/disabled, there's no auto-publish path. Tag push no longer triggers anything — silent no-op, no release.
16. YAML comment before `name:` — YAML spec allows comments anywhere. Parsers accept. But: some CI tools parse workflow filename+name for UI — affected? GitHub Actions handles this fine. Not an edge case.
17. What if the new release entry (section 0) is EMPTY because there are no conventional commits since last tag? Then conventional-changelog-cli `-s` might prepend nothing. head line 1 is still `# Changelog` → guard skips awk. OK.
18. But: what if conventional-changelog-cli prepends ONLY whitespace/empty lines before header? Then line 1 is blank, guard fails, awk runs, no `# Changelog` matches section transition either. Actually wait — `# Changelog` IS somewhere in the file; section 0 collects blank line(s), then at `# Changelog` section becomes 1. OK.
19. What if CHANGELOG.md has CRLF line endings? awk `/^# Changelog/` still matches (CRLF only adds \r to end). `grep -q '^# Changelog'` matches. OK.
20. Commit-version-bump step reads CHANGELOG.md after this — no race; steps are sequential. OK.
21. conventional-changelog behavior: does it always prepend with a blank line separator? If it emits `## [0.11.0]...` directly at byte 0, head -n 1 matches `## [` not `# Changelog`, guard triggers, awk runs — section 0 collects `## [0.11.0]` line. But then: `section==0 && /^# Changelog/` — `## [0.11.0]` does NOT start with `# Changelog`. But `## ` starts with `#` then space then `#`. Regex `^# Changelog` requires `# ` (one hash, space, C). `## [` starts with `#` followed by `#`. Does not match. Good. Line gets added to new_entry. Eventually `# Changelog` appears, transitions. OK.

content.text:
I have enough context to walk every path. Let me analyze systematically.

Key observations:
1. The awk script uses `section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }` — meaning it LOOKS FOR the `# Changelog` line. If input never has `# Changelog` (conventional-changelog-cli output alone), section stays 0 forever → everything is treated as `new_entry`, preamble stays empty, and the final printf emits only the new_entry with NO `# Changelog` title.
2. The top guard `if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'` triggers when the new entry is at top (i.e., what just happened). But what if conventional-changelog prepends something like `# [0.11.0]` (which starts with `#` but not `# Changelog`)? The guard correctly skips; awk runs.
3. What if the new release entry itself CONTAINS a line starting with `# Changelog` in its body (unlikely but possible via commit messages)? Section transitions incorrectly.
4. Section transition from 1→2 uses `/^## \[[0-9]/`. Pre-release tags `[0.10.1-alpha.0]` — the regex matches `[0` so OK. But what about the `[Unreleased]` line itself — `/^## \[[0-9]/` does NOT match `[Unreleased]` so it stays in section 1 (preamble), which is the intent. Good.
5. What if there's NO `## [Unreleased]` anchor? Then section 1 collects from `# Changelog` until it finds a `## [X` line. If preamble is missing Unreleased, the "Unreleased" anchor is lost but the structure still works — but after restore, future runs now lack the Unreleased anchor at expected position.
6. What if `## [Unreleased]` is on line 1 accidentally (no `# Changelog`)? Top guard fails → awk runs, but section never transitions 0→1, entire file is treated as new_entry, preamble stays empty. Output = entire original file with NO `# Changelog`.
7. Empty CHANGELOG.md: head -n 1 returns empty → grep fails → awk runs on empty → printf produces empty string → CHANGELOG.md becomes empty file. Subsequent `grep -iE` step doesn't fail on empty file. `git add CHANGELOG.md` commits empty file.
8. First release ever: if conventional-changelog-cli has never been run, CHANGELOG may not have any `## [X.Y.Z]` history. Section 1 collects everything. Section 2 never triggers. `rest=""`. Preamble includes everything including Unreleased. Order becomes: preamble(full original)+new_entry+"". This is actually fine IF the original had `# Changelog` first. But what if the new conventional-changelog-cli output DIDN'T prepend anything (no new commits)? Then new_entry contains the original `# Changelog` line, section becomes 1 at line 1, preamble gets the rest. Output = "" + new_entry(just "# Changelog\n") + rest(rest of preamble). Actually new_entry = "" (nothing before `# Changelog`), preamble = everything (no `## [X` ever hit), rest = "". This degrades to original. OK.
9. Multiple `# Changelog` lines: only the first transitions 0→1. Subsequent `# Changelog` lines in preamble are just collected. OK.
10. `## [Unreleased]` already present in new_entry from conventional-changelog (unlikely but possible if template customized) — would be collected in section 0 as new_entry, fine.
11. Idempotency under partial previous run: if a previous run crashed AFTER `mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md` but before commit, a re-run sees `# Changelog` at line 1 → no-op. But what if it crashed BEFORE the mv? Then CHANGELOG.md is in the post-conventional-changelog state; awk runs correctly. What if it crashed DURING awk (impossible in practice but) — tmp file exists, next run overwrites it. OK.
12. Preamble-restore output being fed into `Assert no bogus issue refs`: the awk only reorders, doesn't transform. If the new release entry has `fixes [#nonNumeric]` it will be caught. The restore doesn't introduce new matches. But: printf omits a trailing newline? Let's check: `printf "%s%s%s"` — each section ends with `\n` from `$0 "\n"` so final `%s` ends with `\n`. Good. BUT what if original file had no trailing newline — awk reads final line without `\n`, adds `"\n"` during concatenation, restored file ends with `\n`. Diff from original. Minor but not an edge-case bug.
13. `## [Unreleased]` regex: `/^## \[[0-9]/` — what if someone writes `## [unreleased]` (lowercase) or `## [UNRELEASED]`? Still matches neither `[0-9]` start, stays in section 1. Fine.
14. `/^# Changelog/` in section 0 — what if the new release entry contains a heading "# Changelog Highlights" somewhere? That STARTS with `# Changelog` → transitions prematurely. Conventional-changelog-cli normally emits `## [X.Y.Z]` not `# Changelog`, but commit messages embedded in release notes could contain `# Changelog ...` text. 
15. Removed `push: tags: - "v*"` trigger in publish.yaml: if release.yaml is ever deleted/disabled, there's no auto-publish path. Tag push no longer triggers anything — silent no-op, no release.
16. YAML comment before `name:` — YAML spec allows comments anywhere. Parsers accept. But: some CI tools parse workflow filename+name for UI — affected? GitHub Actions handles this fine. Not an edge case.
17. What if the new release entry (section 0) is EMPTY because there are no conventional commits since last tag? Then conventional-changelog-cli `-s` might prepend nothing. head line 1 is still `# Changelog` → guard skips awk. OK.
18. But: what if conventional-changelog-cli prepends ONLY whitespace/empty lines before header? Then line 1 is blank, guard fails, awk runs, no `# Changelog` matches section transition either. Actually wait — `# Changelog` IS somewhere in the file; section 0 collects blank line(s), then at `# Changelog` section becomes 1. OK.
19. What if CHANGELOG.md has CRLF line endings? awk `/^# Changelog/` still matches (CRLF only adds \r to end). `grep -q '^# Changelog'` matches. OK.
20. Commit-version-bump step reads CHANGELOG.md after this — no race; steps are sequential. OK.
21. conventional-changelog behavior: does it always prepend with a blank line separator? If it emits `## [0.11.0]...` directly at byte 0, head -n 1 matches `## [` not `# Changelog`, guard triggers, awk runs — section 0 collects `## [0.11.0]` line. But then: `section==0 && /^# Changelog/` — `## [0.11.0]` does NOT start with `# Changelog`. But `## ` starts with `#` then space then `#`. Regex `^# Changelog` requires `# ` (one hash, space, C). `## [` starts with `#` followed by `#`. Does not match. Good. Line gets added to new_entry. Eventually `# Changelog` appears, transitions. OK. 

Now the edge cases I'll report:

---
---

# Step 3: Triage

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- Be precise. When uncertain between categories, prefer the more conservative classification.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Normalize** findings into a common format. Expected input formats:
   - Adversarial (Blind Hunter): markdown list of descriptions
   - Edge Case Hunter: JSON array with `location`, `trigger_condition`, `guard_snippet`, `potential_consequence` fields
   - Acceptance Auditor: markdown list with title, AC/constraint reference, and evidence

   If a layer's output does not match its expected format, attempt best-effort parsing. Note any parsing issues for the user.

   Convert all to a unified list where each finding has:
   - `id` -- sequential integer
   - `source` -- `blind`, `edge`, `auditor`, or merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`)
   - `title` -- one-line summary
   - `detail` -- full description
   - `location` -- file and line reference (if available)

2. **Deduplicate.** If two or more findings describe the same issue, merge them into one:
   - Use the most specific finding as the base (prefer edge-case JSON with location over adversarial prose).
   - Append any unique detail, reasoning, or location references from the other finding(s) into the surviving `detail` field.
   - Set `source` to the merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`).

3. **Classify** each finding into exactly one bucket:
   - **decision_needed** -- There is an ambiguous choice that requires human input. The code cannot be correctly patched without knowing the user's intent. Only possible if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - **patch** -- Code issue that is fixable without human input. The correct fix is unambiguous.
   - **defer** -- Pre-existing issue not caused by the current change. Real but not actionable now.
   - **dismiss** -- Noise, false positive, or handled elsewhere.

   If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"` and a finding would otherwise be `decision_needed`, reclassify it as `patch` (if the fix is unambiguous) or `defer` (if not).

4. **Drop** all `dismiss` findings. Record the dismiss count for the summary.

5. If `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, report which layers failed before announcing results. If zero findings remain after dropping dismissed AND `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, warn the user that the review may be incomplete rather than announcing a clean review.

6. If zero findings remain after triage (all rejected or none raised): state "✅ Clean review — all layers passed." (Step 3 already warned if any review layers failed via `{failed_layers}`.)


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-04-present.md`

content.content: 1 --- 2 deferred_work_file: '{implementation_artifacts}/deferred-work.md' 3 --- 4 5 # Step 4: Present and Act 6 7 ## RULES 8 9 - YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}` 10 - When `{spec_file}` is set, always write findings to the story file before offering action choices. 11 - `decision-needed` findings must be resolved before handling `patch` findings. 12 13 ## INSTRUCTIONS 14 15 ### 1. Clean review shortcut 16 17 If zero findings remain after triage (all dismissed or none raised): state that and proceed to section 6 (Sprint Status Update). 18 19 ### 2. Write findings to the story file 20 21 If `{spec_file}` exists and contains a Tasks/Subtasks section, append a `### Review Findings` subsection. Write all findings in this order: 22 23 1. **`decision-needed`** findings (unchecked): 24 `- [ ] [Review][Decision] — <Detail>` 25 26 2. **`patch`** findings (unchecked): 27 `- [ ] [Review][Patch] <Title> [<file>:<line>]` 28 29 3. **`defer`** findings (checked off, marked deferred): 30 `- [x] [Review][Defer] <Title> [<file>:<line>] — deferred, pre-existing` 31 32 Also append each `defer` finding to `{deferred_work_file}` under a heading `## Deferred from: code review ({date})`. If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. 33 34 ### 3. Present summary 35 36 Announce what was written: 37 38 > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. 39 40 If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` 41 Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` 42 43 ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings 44 45 If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. 46 47 If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. 48 49 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 50 51 ### 5. Handle `patch` findings 52 53 If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: 54 55 If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): 56 57 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 58 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 59 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 60 > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file 61 > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 62 63 If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: 64 65 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 66 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 67 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 68 > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 69 70 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 71 72 - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. 73 - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. 74 - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. 75 - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. 76 77 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 78 79 **✅ Code review actions complete** 80 81 - Decision-needed resolved: <D> 82 - Patches handled: <P> 83 - Deferred: <W> 84 - Dismissed: <R> 85 86 ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking 87 88 Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. 89 90 #### Determine new status based on review outcome 91 92 - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. 93 - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. 94 95 Save the story file. 96 97 #### Sync sprint-status.yaml 98 99 If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. 100 101 If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 102 103 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

input.description: Inspect release.yaml lines around Restore step

content.content:
sed -i 's/"version": "[^"]*"/"version": "${{ steps.version.outputs.new_version }}"/' .claude-plugin/marketplace.json

      - name: Update CHANGELOG.md
        run: |
          npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s

      - name: Restore CHANGELOG preamble
        run: |
          # conventional-changelog-cli -s prepends the new release entry at byte 0
          # of CHANGELOG.md, pushing "# Changelog" title + preamble + "## [Unreleased]"
          # anchor DOWN below it. Restore the canonical Keep-a-Changelog shape:
          #   # Changelog
          #   (preamble)
          #   ## [Unreleased]
          #   ## [<new release>] (...)
          #   ## [<older releases>] (...)
          # Idempotent: if "# Changelog" is already on line 1, no-op.
          if ! head -n 1 CHANGELOG.md | grep -q '^# Changelog'; then
            awk '
              BEGIN { new_entry=""; preamble=""; rest=""; section=0 }
              # section 0: new release entry prepended by conventional-changelog -s
              # section 1: "# Changelog" title + preamble + "## [Unreleased]" anchor
              # section 2: first released version ("## [X.Y.Z]") onward
              section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }
              section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/ { section=2 }
              { if (section==0) new_entry=new_entry $0 "\n";
                else if (section==1) preamble=preamble $0 "\n";
                else rest=rest $0 "\n" }
              END { printf "%s%s%s", preamble, new_entry, rest }
            ' CHANGELOG.md > CHANGELOG.md.tmp
            mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md
            echo "::notice::Restored CHANGELOG preamble ordering (# Changelog → preamble → [Unreleased] → new release → history)"
          else
            echo "CHANGELOG preamble already at top — no reordering needed"
          fi

      - name: Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG
        run: |
          if grep -iE '(closes|fixes|resolves) \[#[^0-9]' CHANGELOG.md; then
            echo "::error::CHANGELOG.md contains non-numeric '(closes|fixes|resolves) [#...]' refs — aborting release."
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Pre-publish dry-run (catch package validation failures before tag push)
        env:
          NPM_TOKEN: ""

### Review Findings

Code review run 2026-04-21 on diff `535e38f..10c1134` (Patches A/B/C). Three layers: Blind Hunter, Edge Case Hunter, Acceptance Auditor. Triage below; dismissed findings (9) not listed.

**Patch findings** (actionable code fixes — post-merge, via follow-up commit or a bundled Story 3.3 addendum):

- [ ] [Review][Patch] awk silently corrupts CHANGELOG when input lacks `# Changelog` title or is empty [.github/workflows/release.yaml:132-149] — `section==0 && /^# Changelog/ { section=1 }` has no corresponding END assertion; if the title never appears, section stays 0, everything is folded into `new_entry`, preamble is empty, title silently dropped. Compounded by `mv CHANGELOG.md.tmp CHANGELOG.md` with no `[ -s ... ]` guard — an empty/truncated tmp silently clobbers CHANGELOG.md. Fix: add `[ -s CHANGELOG.md ]` precondition; add `END { if (section==0) { print "restore-preamble: # Changelog title not found" > "/dev/stderr"; exit 1 } }`; check `[ -s CHANGELOG.md.tmp ]` before mv. Source: blind+edge (HIGH).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Idempotency guard regex lacks end anchor [.github/workflows/release.yaml:132] — `grep -q '^# Changelog'` matches prefix-lines like `# Changelog-Notes` or `# Changelog (internal)`. Fix: `grep -q '^# Changelog$'` (or `'^# Changelog[[:space:]]*$'` if a trailing space is tolerable). Source: blind+edge (MED).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] New-entry body containing an embedded `# Changelog` line triggers premature section transition [.github/workflows/release.yaml:138] — rare but possible if a commit body contains a line starting `# Changelog`. Fix: anchor the pattern (`/^# Changelog$/`) and add a once-only flag (`section==0 && !seen && /^# Changelog$/ { seen=1; section=1; next }`). Source: edge (MED).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Document the Patch C awk deviation from spec-literal in Completion Notes [spec AC #3 Patch C / story § Completion Notes] — delivered awk is a 2-rule simplification of the spec's 3-rule block, which is a beneficial correction: the spec-literal awk would have produced `# Changelog → preamble → new release → [Unreleased] → history` (inverting the `[Unreleased]`/new-release order stated in the canonical shape on spec lines 42/54-55/80). The shipped code matches the stated canonical shape; the spec's awk was internally contradictory. Fix: append one sentence to § Completion Notes recording the correction so future maintainers don't "fix" the working code back to the buggy spec-literal. Source: auditor (MED).

**Deferred findings** (real concerns, not actionable in Story 3.3):

- [x] [Review][Defer] Non-numeric release version headers (e.g. `## [v0.11.0]`, `## [rc-1]`) bypass section 1→2 transition [.github/workflows/release.yaml:139] — deferred, pre-existing robustness gap; project SemVer scheme always starts with a digit so does not fire today.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Zero-conventional-commit release path (tag + publish proceed with no CHANGELOG entry) [.github/workflows/release.yaml:119] — deferred, pre-existing workflow-level concern outside preamble-restore scope; candidate for Epic 4 release-safety work.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Duplicate `# Changelog` headings produce duplicated output [.github/workflows/release.yaml:138] — deferred, rare pre-existing data-corruption scenario not introduced by this patch.
- [x] [Review][Defer] No post-restore diff/sanity-check for debuggability [.github/workflows/release.yaml:149] — deferred, diagnostic nice-to-have; not a correctness issue.
- [x] [Review][Defer] No end-to-end assertion that `release.yaml` owns the `v*` tag-push path now that `publish.yaml` no longer does [.github/workflows/publish.yaml:4-5] — deferred, operational safety concern belongs to Epic 4 RELEASING.md rollback playbook.

## Dev Notes

/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/deferred-work.md
- **Story 5.2 needs a one-time hand-bump path to start the v1.0.0 prerelease cycle** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:70-75`. Current `version_bump: alpha|beta|rc` logic uses `npm version prerelease --preid=...` which produces `0.10.1-alpha.0` from `0.10.0` — NOT `1.0.0-alpha.0`. To cut `v1.0.0-rc.1` (Story 5.2's plan), a one-time manual bump is needed first: `npm version 1.0.0-rc.0 --no-git-tag-version`, commit-and-push-to-main via PR, then dispatch `release.yaml` with `version_bump: rc` (which will increment to `1.0.0-rc.1`). Alternatively, extend `release.yaml` with `premajor-rc` choice option — but that bloats workflow surface for a one-time event. **Action for Story 5.1 (readiness audit):** decide between hand-bump procedure (documented in `docs/RELEASING.md § Cutting v1.0.0-rc.1`) and workflow extension. Recommendation: hand-bump (lighter surface, one-time event). Tracked from Story 3.1 code review.

## Deferred from: implementation of 3-2-validate-release-yaml-via-alpha-test-cut-from-feature-branch (2026-04-21)

- **[HIGH] CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` anchor lifecycle is structurally broken by `conventional-changelog-cli -s` append mode** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:87-89` (`Update CHANGELOG.md` step) against `CHANGELOG.md`. Story 3.2's alpha cut (the designated live validator for this deferred-work from Story 3.1 review) produced concrete evidence: the preset prepends the new release entry at byte 0 of the infile, pushing the `# Changelog` title, Keep-a-Changelog preamble, and `## [Unreleased]` anchor DOWN below the new entry. Post-cut shape of `git show v0.10.1-alpha.0:CHANGELOG.md`: `line 1: ## [0.10.1-alpha.0]...`, `line 118: # Changelog`, `line 120: All notable changes...`, `line 122: ## [Unreleased]`, `line 124: ## [0.10.0]...`. Every subsequent release compounds the disorder. **Decision (Story 3.2 close-out, user-approved):** defer the fix to Story 3.3's merge PR (not Story 3.2 scope expansion). Story 3.3 should add a post-generation step to `release.yaml` that restores the preamble block to the top of the file after the preset runs — either via a small Python helper, a sed/awk pipeline, or switching to a generation approach that honors the `[Unreleased]` anchor as the insertion point. If Story 3.3 does NOT bundle this, the FIRST main-branch release cut will ship with the malformed shape and every release thereafter will compound it. Story 5.1 (pre-v1.0.0 audit) is the later backstop if Story 3.3 punts. Evidence: Story 3.2 Dev Agent Record § Debug Log References § AC #10 lifecycle Q1/Q2/Q3 answers.
- **[MED] Dev agent wrote `AC #N` in commit bodies; preset parsed `#N` as GH issue refs** — `0.10.1-alpha.0` CHANGELOG entry (orphaned commit `2a57dcbd`; also preserved in the published tarball's CHANGELOG). Four defect-fix commits (`f449c67`, `cbc7851`, `de2334b`, `cae5861`) had bodies referencing `AC #11` / `AC #12` meaning Acceptance Criteria — the conventionalcommits preset interpreted these as GitHub issue references and emitted `closes [#11]` / `closes [#12]` links in the CHANGELOG. The `Assert no bogus issue refs` guard correctly did NOT fire (refs are numeric; the guard targets the Story 2.2 class-of-bug of non-numeric refs). Cosmetic defect in a throwaway alpha entry. **Process fix for all future dev agents:** never write `AC #N` in commit bodies when the repo uses conventional-changelog tooling — write `AC N`, `AC-N`, or `acceptance criterion N`. Remediation options: (a) hand-correct the 0.10.1-alpha.0 CHANGELOG entry in Story 5.1's audit pass, (b) accept as historical artifact since AC #9 preserves the alpha as evidence only (no consumer will install `@alpha` intentionally pre-v1.0.0). Recommendation: (b) — low-value edit with npm-side mutability constraints (published tarball immutable).
- **[LOW] `f449c67` (first failed `--force` fix attempt) is dead-code in the feat/release-workflow chain** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml`. The `--force` approach was fully superseded by `cbc7851`'s side-prefix install; `f449c67`'s step body was overwritten one commit later. Preserving per Story 3.2 AC #11 "no amend / no force-push" rule — the commit chain is the audit trail of the debugging path. Story 3.3's merge PR will carry it as noise in the commit-list diff view. **Action:** none; Story 3.3 reviewer should be aware the 4 `fix(release):` commits include one superseded intermediate. Acceptable cost for audit integrity.
- **[LOW] GitHub-hosted runner's bundled npm (Node 22.22.2 → npm 10.9.7) falls below the trusted-publisher floor of 11.5.1** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml` `Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing` step. Side-prefix install resolves it but adds ~15s to every release run. Future optimization: when a Node 22.x release ships bundled npm ≥11.5.1 by default, conditionally skip the bootstrap step (`if: npm_version < 11.5.1`). Not blocking. Tracked as a Story 5.1 NFR1 (15-min CI target) cycle-time data point.
- **[INFO] Node.js 20 deprecation warnings on every run for `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4`, `astral-sh/setup-uv@v6`, `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`** — all repo workflows. GitHub forcing Node 24 by 2026-06-02; Node 20 removed 2026-09-16. Repo-wide upgrade scope, not Story 3.2. Two paths: rely on action maintainers to publish Node-24-compatible versions before the deadline (most will), or set `FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true` at workflow level now. Defer to a post-v1.0.0 maintenance story.
- **[INFO] `astral-sh/setup-uv@v6` cache-miss warning on every release run** — `release.yaml:54-57`. "No file matched to ... requirements.txt ... pyproject.toml ... uv.lock". Repo has Python test files (pytest via uv) but no Python lockfile, so uv's cache never fills. `npm test` (including Python tests) still passes; cache never invalidates. Low-priority maintenance cleanup — add a `pyproject.toml` + `uv.lock` in the Python test dir, or silence the warning via `setup-uv`'s cache-dependency-glob input. Defer.

## Deferred from: code review of 3-2-validate-release-yaml-via-alpha-test-cut-from-feature-branch (2026-04-21)

- **Substring-based `alpha|beta|rc` tag classification, now doubled by dry-run mirror** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:117-123` (dry-run) + `:184-190` (real publish). The Story 3.2 fix `de2334b` added the dry-run case-chain byte-parallel to Story 3.1's publish chain — correct for AC #5 consistency, but the substring pattern (`*"alpha"*`, `*"beta"*`, `*"rc"*`) can misclassify unusual semver strings. Concrete example from the spec (line 739): a future `1.0.0-march.1` would match `*"rc"*` in the case-chain. Proper fix is semver-aware parsing or anchoring to the prerelease identifier segment. Coordinated change to both chains. Track with Story 3.3 or split story.
- **Empty `$VERSION` silently routes to `@latest`** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:117-123` (dry-run) + `:184-190` (real publish). Neither case-chain guards `[[ -z "$VERSION" ]]`. If `steps.version.outputs.new_version` is ever empty (bump step failed without aborting, corrupted `package.json` mid-job, etc.), `TAG=latest` and the package publishes to `@latest`. Coordinated `[[ -z "$VERSION" ]] && exit 1` guard belongs with the substring-collision fix above.
- **`npm ci` regenerates `.husky/_/` wrappers on CI; `HUSKY: "0"` is the sole safety net** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:75` (`Install dependencies` runs `npm ci` which fires the `"prepare": "husky || exit 0"` lifecycle) + `:125` (`Commit version bump` which would trip `.husky/commit-msg` without `HUSKY=0`). Defense-in-depth: add `-c core.hooksPath=/dev/null` or `--no-verify` to the `git commit` call, so a future regression that drops the job-level env (e.g. matrix split, reusable-workflow extraction) does not silently reintroduce the commit-msg failure on `entire` absence.
- **`Commit version bump` uses non-conventional prefix `release:`** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:125`. Standard conventional-commits types are `feat/fix/chore/docs/style/refactor/perf/test/build/ci/revert`; `release:` is non-canonical. Harmless under `HUSKY=0` (commit-msg hook skipped), but doubles the failure surface if that env is ever dropped. Trivial rename to `chore(release):` — combine with the `--no-verify` defense-in-depth change above.
- **Git tag pushed before npm publish — OIDC failure orphans the tag on `origin`** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:161-167` (`Create and push tag`) vs `:181` (`Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing`). Pre-existing from Story 3.1; Story 3.2 does not introduce but now the `--tag` derivation change advertises a dry-run gate that (correctly) does NOT cover OIDC failures. If OIDC handshake fails after tag-push, `v$VERSION` exists on GitHub without an npm artifact. Belongs with Story 4.1 rollback playbook or a dedicated hardening story (reorder to tag-after-publish, or add a compensating tag-delete step on publish failure).
- **Feature-branch alpha-cut tag commits are orphaned on branch deletion** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:167` (unconditional `git push origin "v..."`) vs `:170` (conditional `Push commit to main` gated on `github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'`). On `workflow_dispatch` from a feature branch (Story 3.2 alpha-cut pattern), the `v0.10.1-alpha.0` tag points to a commit that exists only on the feature branch. Post-merge branch deletion may GC that commit depending on GitHub retention; `git describe --tags --abbrev=0` in later runs may fail or pick the wrong previous tag. Interacts with Story 4.1 rollback semantics and with FR / NFR around release-history lineage. Track with Story 3.3 or Story 4.1.
- **Execution-quality note — each failed run required a fresh user-approval click at the env gate.** In a solo-maintainer context (`prevent_self_review: false`), this costs ~5–10s of maintainer time per retry. In a post-Epic-1-Carry-forward-#3 world (second reviewer onboarded, `prevent_self_review: true` flipped), each failed run would require the second reviewer's re-approval — materially increasing iteration cost on workflow defects. NFR1 (15-min CI cycle) re-evaluation for Story 5.1 should account for this. Not a workflow defect; a real-world cost to surface for audit.

## Deferred from: code review of 3-3-merge-release-yaml-to-main-and-retain-legacy-workflows-as-inert-backup (2026-04-21)

- **Non-numeric release version headers bypass section 1→2 transition** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:139`. The awk rule `section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/` only transitions on `[<digit>`, so hypothetical future tag schemes like `## [v0.11.0]` (with a `v` prefix) or `## [rc-1]` (no leading digit) would be absorbed into the preamble accumulator and the new release would be misplaced. Current SemVer convention always starts with a digit (including pre-release identifiers like `0.10.1-alpha.0`), so this does not fire today. Robustness-only; broaden to `/^## \[/ && !/^## \[Unreleased\]/` when a future story touches this step.
- **Zero-conventional-commit release path — workflow proceeds with empty changelog entry** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:119` (Update CHANGELOG.md step). If there are no conventional commits since the last tag, `conventional-changelog-cli -s` prepends nothing; the preamble-restore guard correctly no-ops, but the workflow continues to tag + publish with no CHANGELOG entry for the new version. Upstream concern (belongs to Update CHANGELOG, not Restore Preamble); candidate for an Epic 4 release-safety hardening pass — detect empty prepend and `exit 1`.
- **Duplicate `# Changelog` headings in malformed input produce duplicated preamble output** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:138`. Rare pre-existing data-corruption scenario (e.g., from a botched merge): the first `# Changelog` line transitions 0→1 correctly; any additional `# Changelog` lines are swept into the preamble accumulator and duplicated in the output. Not introduced by Patch C — any reorder against that input shape is ambiguous. Out of scope until we see one in the wild.
- **No post-restore diff/sanity-check step for CI debuggability** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:149`. Purely diagnostic — a `head -20 CHANGELOG.md` or `git diff CHANGELOG.md | head` after the restore would speed root-causing if the `Assert no bogus issue refs` step ever fails on a mutated file. Not a correctness issue. Bundle with any future release-workflow debugging pass.
- **No end-to-end assertion that `release.yaml` owns the `v*` tag-push path now that `publish.yaml` does not** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:4-5` removed `push: tags: - "v*"` (neutralized per Patch A). If `release.yaml` is ever disabled, deleted, or reverted, a `v*` tag push would now silently no-op (no publish, no GitHub Release, no tag-to-artifact linkage). Belongs to Epic 4's RELEASING.md six-scenario rollback playbook — the "release.yaml disabled" scenario needs a runbook entry and monitoring signal. Low probability, high-consequence fallback gap.

- **Git tag pushed before npm publish — OIDC failure orphans the tag on `origin`** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:161-167` (`Create and push tag`) vs `:181` (`Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing`). Pre-existing from Story 3.1; Story 3.2 does not introduce but now the `--tag` derivation change advertises a dry-run gate that (correctly) does NOT cover OIDC failures. If OIDC handshake fails after tag-push, `v$VERSION` exists on GitHub without an npm artifact. Belongs with Story 4.1 rollback playbook or a dedicated hardening story (reorder to tag-after-publish, or add a compensating tag-delete step on publish failure).
- **Feature-branch alpha-cut tag commits are orphaned on branch deletion** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:167` (unconditional `git push origin "v..."`) vs `:170` (conditional `Push commit to main` gated on `github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'`). On `workflow_dispatch` from a feature branch (Story 3.2 alpha-cut pattern), the `v0.10.1-alpha.0` tag points to a commit that exists only on the feature branch. Post-merge branch deletion may GC that commit depending on GitHub retention; `git describe --tags --abbrev=0` in later runs may fail or pick the wrong previous tag. Interacts with Story 4.1 rollback semantics and with FR / NFR around release-history lineage. Track with Story 3.3 or Story 4.1.
- **Execution-quality note — each failed run required a fresh user-approval click at the env gate.** In a solo-maintainer context (`prevent_self_review: false`), this costs ~5–10s of maintainer time per retry. In a post-Epic-1-Carry-forward-#3 world (second reviewer onboarded, `prevent_self_review: true` flipped), each failed run would require the second reviewer's re-approval — materially increasing iteration cost on workflow defects. NFR1 (15-min CI cycle) re-evaluation for Story 5.1 should account for this. Not a workflow defect; a real-world cost to surface for audit.

## Deferred from: code review of 3-3-merge-release-yaml-to-main-and-retain-legacy-workflows-as-inert-backup (2026-04-21)

- **Non-numeric release version headers bypass section 1→2 transition** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:139`. The awk rule `section==1 && /^## \[[0-9]/` only transitions on `[<digit>`, so hypothetical future tag schemes like `## [v0.11.0]` (with a `v` prefix) or `## [rc-1]` (no leading digit) would be absorbed into the preamble accumulator and the new release would be misplaced. Current SemVer convention always starts with a digit (including pre-release identifiers like `0.10.1-alpha.0`), so this does not fire today. Robustness-only; broaden to `/^## \[/ && !/^## \[Unreleased\]/` when a future story touches this step.
- **Zero-conventional-commit release path — workflow proceeds with empty changelog entry** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:119` (Update CHANGELOG.md step). If there are no conventional commits since the last tag, `conventional-changelog-cli -s` prepends nothing; the preamble-restore guard correctly no-ops, but the workflow continues to tag + publish with no CHANGELOG entry for the new version. Upstream concern (belongs to Update CHANGELOG, not Restore Preamble); candidate for an Epic 4 release-safety hardening pass — detect empty prepend and `exit 1`.
- **Duplicate `# Changelog` headings in malformed input produce duplicated preamble output** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:138`. Rare pre-existing data-corruption scenario (e.g., from a botched merge): the first `# Changelog` line transitions 0→1 correctly; any additional `# Changelog` lines are swept into the preamble accumulator and duplicated in the output. Not introduced by Patch C — any reorder against that input shape is ambiguous. Out of scope until we see one in the wild.
- **No post-restore diff/sanity-check step for CI debuggability** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml:149`. Purely diagnostic — a `head -20 CHANGELOG.md` or `git diff CHANGELOG.md | head` after the restore would speed root-causing if the `Assert no bogus issue refs` step ever fails on a mutated file. Not a correctness issue. Bundle with any future release-workflow debugging pass.
- **No end-to-end assertion that `release.yaml` owns the `v*` tag-push path now that `publish.yaml` does not** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:4-5` removed `push: tags: - "v*"` (neutralized per Patch A). If `release.yaml` is ever disabled, deleted, or reverted, a `v*` tag push would now silently no-op (no publish, no GitHub Release, no tag-to-artifact linkage). Belongs to Epic 4's RELEASING.md six-scenario rollback playbook — the "release.yaml disabled" scenario needs a runbook entry and monitoring signal. Low probability, high-consequence fallback gap.

Findings persisted. Presenting summary.

**Code review complete.** 0 `decision-needed`, 4 `patch`, 5 `defer`, 10 dismissed as noise. Findings written to the Review Findings section in `3-3-merge-release-yaml-to-main-and-retain-legacy-workflows-as-inert-backup.md`; deferred items also logged in `deferred-work.md`.

**Patch findings (4):**
1. **HIGH** — awk silently corrupts CHANGELOG without `# Changelog` title + `mv` clobber with empty tmp `[release.yaml:132-149]` *(blind+edge)*
2. **MED** — idempotency guard regex lacks `$` end-anchor `[release.yaml:132]` *(blind+edge)*
3. **MED** — embedded `# Changelog` line in new-entry body triggers premature section transition `[release.yaml:138]` *(edge)*
4. **MED** — document Patch C awk spec-deviation in Completion Notes (delivered code corrects a bug in the spec's literal awk) *(auditor)*

**Note on context:** PR #193 already merged to `main` as `14cedfe`. Any code fixes apply to live `main` and would land via a follow-up commit (bundled addendum or a small hardening commit on `feat/release-workflow`).

**How would you like to handle the 4 `patch` findings?**
0. **Batch-apply all** — auto-fix every non-controversial patch
1. **Fix them automatically** — I apply fixes now
2. **Leave as action items** — they are in the story file
3. **Walk through each** — show details before deciding

**HALT** — reply with only the number.
